CSCE 665 Syllabus

Section 600 (30874)

Advanced Networking and Security

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: F

Start Time: 1:30PM

End Time: 4:30PM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 598

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Guofei Gu

Email:  guofei@tamu.edu

Office Location:  Room 239 Peterson

Phone:  9798452475

Office Hours

3-4pm Wednesday or by appointment

Preferred Contact Method

Email: guofei@cse.tamu.edu

Catalog Description

Advanced Networking and Security. (3-0). Credit 3. Security aspects of various

network protocols including investigation and tool development using "live"

machines and networks. Prerequisites: Graduate classification and approval of

instructor.

Additional Course Details

This course will introduce various modern topics in computer and network security. It will

provide a thorough grounding in computer and network security suitable for those interested

in conducting research in this area, as well as students more broadly interested in real-world

security issues/techniques. Topics may span (but not limited to):

Basic network & system security: e.g., software security, malware, botnet, intrusion

detection

security issues/techniques. Topics may span (but not limited to):

Basic network & system security: e.g., software security, malware, botnet, intrusion

detection

Security in a software-defined world: e.g., SDN/NFV, Cloud, 5G/Edge, Vehicle security

Mobile & IoT security: e.g., smartphone security, Alexa and voice app security

Web and Web3 security: e.g., traditional web security, spam, Web3 (DeFi, blockchain)

security

AI/LLM security: e.g., LLM-based security, adversarial Learning / backdoor, advanced

applications with AI

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Graduate classification and approval of instructor.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Analyze the complex trade-offs and architectural tensions inherent in securing modern

distributed systems and networks.

Synthesize fundamental security principles and mechanisms to design robust

defenses against sophisticated threat models.

Critique state-of-the-art security literature to identify open problems, methodological

flaws, and emerging trends in the field.

Demonstrate a practical working knowledge of offensive and/or defensive techniques

to contextualize theoretical concepts.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

Various papers

Notes:

There is no required textbook. Most readings will be from research papers in top security

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

Various papers

Notes:

There is no required textbook. Most readings will be from research papers in top security

conferences and journals (listed below).

Grading Policy

Paper mini-review: 25% (No AI tools such as ChatGPT are allowed to use in the

review writing)

Class participation and presentation: 10%

Mini research project: 65%

There is no mid-term or final exam.

There will be bonus points for EXCELLENT mini-research projects.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

All late submissions will automatically lose 20% points per delayed day. Submissions five

days after the deadline will NOT be accepted (unless you get permission from the instructor).
